questions.question: Why must all packets of one client request be sent to the same server in a server farm?
options: To reduce total bandwidth on the front-end switch
Because TCP connection state lives on one server, and splitting packets breaks it
So the back-end database only sees one query per request
To keep DNS cache entries consistent across resolvers
questions.explanation: A Web request is a TCP connection. If the front end splits packets of one connection across servers, no single server holds the TCP state, and the connection breaks.

questions.question: A front end uses IP-hash with 16 servers, selecting a server by the last 4 bits of the source IP. An entire company NATs all its users to one public IP. What is the observable failure?
options: All users are load balanced evenly across the 16 servers
Every user from that company lands on the same single server, causing a hot spot
The front end rejects the packets because the source IP is not unique
DNS returns a rotating list that fixes the imbalance
questions.explanation: IP-hash maps one source IP to exactly one server. When a whole organization is NATed to one public IP, all its users hash to the same server, collapsing the farm into a hot spot while the other 15 servers sit idle.

questions.question: What makes a server farm more reliable than a single server, and what front-end behavior is required for that reliability?
options: Faster CPUs; the front end must cache HTTP responses
Redundant power supplies; the front end must use DNS rotation
Health checks that detect failed servers and drain traffic away from them
A larger back-end database; the front end must broadcast every request
questions.explanation: Reliability comes from redundancy plus active health checks: the front end notices when a server has failed and stops sending it requests, so the remaining servers absorb the load instead of failing with the dead one.

questions.question: The source calls the front end a 'middlebox' that violates layering. What does this mean, and what is the practical risk?
options: It routes packets, so it is an IP router and nothing more
It inspects transport and application headers (TCP, HTTP cookies) to make decisions, which breaks if higher layers change
It encrypts traffic, so it must terminate TLS for every connection
It is physically in the middle of the rack, so cabling limits its speed
questions.explanation: A middlebox inspects headers of layers it does not belong to (e.g., a switch reading TCP/HTTP fields). This is useful but fragile: a higher-layer change, such as HTTP/3 over QUIC, can break a front end that relied on TCP headers.
